patterns.costImplications: Data Factory pipeline runs: $0.50 per execution. Fabric compute for Notebook purge jobs: standard rates. Storage savings from deletion: 10-30% reduction in employee data volume.

patterns.name: Statistical Anonymization for HR Analytics
patterns.summary: Implements k-anonymity and differential privacy techniques to ensure published HR analytics cannot identify individuals through aggregation attacks or frequency analysis.
patterns.description: K-anonymity ensures that any combination of quasi-identifiers (age, department, salary band) appears in at least k records, preventing record linkage attacks. Differential privacy adds carefully calibrated noise to query results, enabling accurate aggregate statistics while preventing inference of individual values. For HR analytics, aggregation-only views enforce k-anonymity by requiring HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5 clauses on all queries, preventing disclosure of rare populations (e.g., the sole executive in a location). Noise injection adds Laplace or Gaussian noise proportional to sensitivity, with epsilon (privacy budget) controlling noise magnitude. Views in Fabric Warehouse or SQL Analytics Endpoint implement these controls. PySpark notebooks implement noise injection for ad-hoc analyses. Practical applications include publishing salary bands by role (not individuals), showing headcount by department (minimum 5 per group), and publishing engagement scores by location-function groups.
pros: Provides formal mathematical guarantees against re-identification attacks, meeting GDPR and other privacy regulations' proportionality requirements.
Enables publishing aggregate HR analytics to broad audiences (all employees, board, public) without risk of individual inference.
Can be implemented as database views and functions, integrating seamlessly into existing analytics without changing consuming applications.
cons: K-anonymity vulnerable to semantic attacks if quasi-identifiers are not carefully chosen; requires domain expertise to identify all identifying combinations.
Noise injection reduces statistical utility; high privacy budgets (epsilon) may allow inference while low budgets (epsilon <0.1) add significant noise.
Maintaining k-anonymity becomes harder as dataset grows and rare populations appear; may force conservative suppression of legitimate insights.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Identify quasi-identifiers in HR data (age, salary band, department, location, tenure, role). 2. Create aggregation-only views with HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5. 3. Test for k=5 anonymity: SELECT department, role, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department, role HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5. 4. For salary analytics, implement noise injection in Python: create base aggregate (SELECT department, avg(salary) as avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department), calculate sensitivity (max salary difference), generate Laplace noise with epsilon=0.5, add noise to aggregates. 5. Create security principal with view-only permissions to anonymized views. 6. Publish anonymized views via Power BI semantic model with field-level security. 7. Document epsilon budgets and k values for each published analysis.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish privacy analytics working group with data scientists, privacy officers, and HR stakeholders. Document quasi-identifier sets and privacy threat model per analysis. Set epsilon budgets based on acceptable privacy-utility tradeoff. Review all published aggregates for k-anonymity before release. Track total epsilon consumption across all published analyses to prevent privacy budget exhaustion. Implement technical controls preventing ad-hoc SQL query access to raw employee data.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Publishing company-wide salary equity analysis to all employees showing average salary by department-level-location, with k=10 minimum group size, enabling transparency without revealing individual salaries.
Generating anonymized performance distribution histograms for board review with noise injection ensuring no distribution shape discloses individual outliers.
Creating recruitment funnel analytics published externally showing aggregate conversion rates by source-role with k=5 minimums, preventing inference of individual candidate outcomes.

patterns.name: Audit Log Export and SIEM Integration
patterns.summary: Forwards Fabric activity logs and Power BI audit logs to Azure Sentinel for centralized SOC monitoring, anomaly detection, and forensic investigation.
patterns.description: Audit log integration ensures all Fabric and Power BI activities (data access, semantic model changes, sharing, export, deletion) are captured, centralized, and analyzed for security incidents. Fabric activity logs (workspace creation, item access, refresh execution, query patterns) flow to Log Analytics Workspace. Power BI activity logs (report view, dataset refresh, sharing changes, export) are forwarded to the Unified Audit Log via the Office 365 Management API. Azure Sentinel consumes these logs, applies correlation rules to detect anomalies (bulk data export, after-hours access, privilege escalation), enriches with identity and threat intelligence, and triggers incidents for SOC investigation. Custom KQL (Kusto Query Language) queries detect HR-specific threats: unusual bulk report exports, access to sensitive employee data by non-HR users, policy violation patterns. Retention ensures logs are available for 1-2 years for forensic analysis and compliance audits.
pros: Centralizes audit logs from Fabric and Power BI in single searchable repository enabling cross-system correlation and forensic investigations.
Detects insider threats and compliance violations in real-time through rule-based alerting and anomaly detection, enabling rapid response.
Provides evidence trail for regulatory audits and incident response, meeting SOX, HIPAA, and other audit requirements.
cons: Azure Sentinel ingestion costs scale with log volume; busy Fabric environments can generate 1000+ GB/month of logs, increasing licensing costs.
Lag between activity and Sentinel processing (typically 1-5 minutes) means real-time detection is limited; historical detection takes longer.
False positive rates in correlation rules can overwhelm SOC; requires significant tuning and stakeholder coordination.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Create Log Analytics Workspace in Azure. 2. Enable Fabric activity logging: Workspace Settings > Audit and Compliance > Enable Activity Logging. 3. Create Data Connector in Log Analytics: Fabric Activity > Diagnostic Settings > Send to Log Analytics Workspace. 4. Enable Power BI audit logging: Power BI Admin Portal > Audit and Compliance > Turn on audit log search. 5. Configure Office 365 Management API in Sentinel to consume Unified Audit Log. 6. In Azure Sentinel, create Data Connectors for Office 365 and Log Analytics. 7. Create KQL detection rules: query for bulk exports (>1000 rows), access to SSN columns, after-hours queries. 8. Configure Incidents from detections with SOAR playbook triggers. 9. Create Power BI dashboard for audit KPIs: daily active users, top data consumers, failed access attempts.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish Security Operations Center (SOC) workflow for Sentinel incidents related to HR data. Define escalation procedures for confirmed security incidents (e.g., data exfiltration). Implement log retention policies: 6 months hot storage, 2 years cold archive. Conduct quarterly reviews of detection rules to reduce false positives. Require formal change management approval for queries accessing sensitive audit data. Ensure audit log access is restricted to security team and select compliance officers.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Real-time detection of bulk export of executive compensation reports (>100 rows) to external email domains, triggering SOC investigation into potential salary data exfiltration.
Anomaly detection identifying that a non-HR user (e.g., IT service account) is accessing employee SSN and salary columns during off-hours, indicating potential credential compromise.
Forensic investigation of a departing manager's last-day activities: queried employee performance data, shared compensation history report with personal email, then deleted workspace link.

patterns.name: Data Subject Request Fulfillment Pipeline
patterns.summary: Automates GDPR/PIPEDA data subject rights (access and erasure) with workflows using Purview data lineage, soft-delete mechanisms, and verification processes.
patterns.description: Data Subject Requests (DSR) are formal legal requests from individuals to access (GDPR Article 15) or erase (GDPR Article 17, right-to-be-forgotten) their personal data. Manual DSR handling is error-prone, slow, and costly. Automated DSR fulfillment pipelines use Microsoft Purview Data Map to identify all systems containing an individual's data, orchestrate retrieval or deletion via Data Factory, perform soft-delete in Fabric Lakehouse with audit trail, notify requestor, and verify completion. For HR analytics, DSR requests require identifying employee records across Lakehouse, data warehouse, Power BI datasets, and archived systems. Soft-delete (marking records inactive) enables 30-day appeal period before hard-delete. Lineage mapping shows which analytics and reports are impacted by erasure. Workflow includes validation steps preventing accidental bulk deletion.
pros: Automates time-consuming manual search and deletion, reducing DSR fulfillment time from weeks to days and reducing legal/compliance costs.
Uses Purview lineage to ensure complete erasure across all systems; manual processes frequently miss copies or derived data.
Provides audit trail proving compliance with GDPR/PIPEDA deadlines (30 days for access, 30 days for erasure), supporting regulatory defense.
cons: Incomplete or inaccurate Purview Data Map can result in missed data systems and non-compliance; requires continuous curation.
Soft-delete relies on application logic filtering deleted records; risk of query bugs exposing deleted data if filters are incorrect.
Determining which analytic derivatives (e.g., aggregates, models, exports) must be updated/deleted is complex and data-dependent.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Create DSR request intake form (SharePoint form or Power Apps) capturing: requestor name, email, request type (access/erasure), date. 2. Create Data Factory pipeline: Step 1 - Query Purview Data Map API for assets containing individual's data (e.g., Employee.EmployeeId = 12345). Step 2 - Generate list of impacted systems/tables. Step 3 - For access requests, export data to encrypted file in secure SharePoint. For erasure requests, mark records with is_dsr_deleted=1, log deletion timestamp/reason. Step 4 - Wait 30 days for appeals. Step 5 - Hard-delete (VACUUM, UPDATE DELETE). Step 6 - Notify requestor and compliance team. 7. Implement controls: require approvals for bulk erasure, audit all DSR operations, verify deletion completeness.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish DSR response team with legal, HR, privacy, and data teams. Document DSR procedures and compliance timelines per jurisdiction. Implement technical access controls preventing unauthorized DSR request manipulation. Maintain DSR request audit log with decision and verification steps. Conduct quarterly audits to verify soft-deleted records are truly inaccessible to analytics. Maintain appeal process for 30-day grace period post-deletion request.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Automated fulfillment of GDPR access request from Canadian employee: Purview identifies data in Lakehouse employee table, warehouse HRIS system, Power BI reports. Pipeline exports to encrypted PDF in 5 days, within GDPR 30-day SLA.

Right-to-erasure request from terminated EU employee: soft-delete removes from all active tables, historical audit trails remain but employee salary/SSN are masked from all analytics, 30-day appeal period prevents accidental permanent deletion.

Cross-border DSR for US employee working in EU office: pipeline identifies data in US Lake House capacity (compliant with GDPR data residency because US subject to standard contractual clauses), executes soft-delete with audit trail.

patterns.name: Disaster Recovery and Geo-Redundancy
patterns.summary: Configures OneLake with zone-redundant storage (ZRS) and geo-replication to paired Azure region, with manual failover procedures and multi-geo capacity for data residency.
patterns.description: Disaster recovery ensures business continuity when regional outages occur. OneLake Zone-Redundant Storage (ZRS) replicates data across three availability zones within a region, protecting against zone-level failures. Geo-replication asynchronously copies data to a paired region (e.g., US East 2 to US Central 1), protecting against regional disasters. Fabric Capacity can be provisioned in multiple regions to enable manual failover. For HR analytics, disaster recovery ensures employee data is always available and compliant with data residency laws (e.g., Canadian data stays in Canada region). Multi-geo capacity configuration enables capacity to be deployed in specific regions. Backup procedures include daily snapshots exported to secure storage. Manual failover involves updating connection strings and repointing workspaces to failover capacity in alternate region. Recovery Time Objective (RTO) for manual failover is 4-8 hours; automated failover via service mesh future capability targets RTO <15 minutes.
pros: ZRS provides automatic replication without application changes, protecting against zone and data center failures with no additional cost.
Geo-replication enables regional compliance (e.g., Canadian data in Canada) and disaster recovery to another region, meeting business continuity SLAs.
Manual failover procedures are tested and documented, enabling rapid recovery with minimal training.
cons: Geo-replication introduces asynchronous delay (minutes to hours); recent data changes may not be replicated if failover occurs immediately after write.
Manual failover requires human action and coordination, typically taking 4-8 hours; automated failover is not yet available.
Multi-region Fabric Capacity costs increase significantly; maintaining hot-hot failover doubles capacity costs vs. cold standby.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Enable ZRS on OneLake workspace: Workspace Settings > Storage Redundancy > Zone-Redundant Storage. 2. Configure geo-replication: Workspace Settings > Disaster Recovery > Enable Geo-Replication to paired region (e.g., East 2 to Central 1). 3. Create Data Factory pipeline for daily snapshot export: copy all Lakehouse tables to Azure Storage Account in alternate region. 4. Document failover runbook: 1) Declare disaster, 2) Notify stakeholders, 3) Verify failover region readiness, 4) Update Fabric workspace connection to failover capacity, 5) Repoint Power BI datasets to failover semantic models, 6) Validate data integrity in failover region. 5. Conduct quarterly disaster recovery drill: simulate regional failure, execute failover, validate analytics in alternate region, measure RTO. 6. Maintain failover capacity in standby mode or scale down when not in use.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish disaster recovery committee with IT, business continuity, and HR stakeholders. Define RTO (Recovery Time Objective) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective) per service level agreement. Document failover procedures and roles. Test failover procedures quarterly with full team participation. Monitor geo-replication lag and alert if lag exceeds SLA. Maintain separate credentials for failover region. Ensure external audit of disaster recovery controls annually.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Regional outage in US East region: automatic ZRS failover within same region (1-2 minutes). If both East zones unavailable, manual failover to US Central capacity (4 hours RTO) ensures payroll analytics continue.

Compliance requirement for Canadian employee data to remain in Canada: Canadian Fabric Capacity with geo-replication to alternate Canadian region, ensuring data never leaves Canada even during failover.
Financial services regulatory requirement for near-zero RPO: async geo-replication has 30-minute lag, so daily snapshot export to alternate region ensures RPO <24 hours.

referenceLinks.title: Power BI high availability and disaster recovery

patterns.costImplications: ZRS: no additional cost. Geo-replication: $0.02/GB/month for replication. Failover capacity: $16/hour standby, $96/hour active (Premium P1).

patterns.name: Network Isolation with Private Endpoints
patterns.summary: Implements Azure Private Endpoints for Fabric Capacity and managed private endpoints for Spark/compute, ensuring traffic flows only over private VNet and preventing internet exposure.
patterns.description: Private Endpoints eliminate internet routes for Fabric services, instead routing all traffic through Azure VNet private network. Fabric Capacity (compute and storage) uses Private Endpoints to ensure no data traverses public internet. Managed Private Endpoints for Spark Compute enable secure communication with private data sources (SQL Database in VNet, storage accounts with firewall enabled). For secure HR analytics implementations, network isolation ensures employee data never transits public internet. Network security groups (NSGs) can further restrict traffic by source/destination IP. VNet integration enables hybrid connectivity: on-premises HR systems can securely connect to Fabric via ExpressRoute. Outbound traffic can be forced through proxy/firewall for inspection. Azure Private Link services provide 99.99% availability.
pros: Eliminates internet exposure of Fabric endpoints, significantly reducing attack surface and complying with network isolation requirements.
Enables hybrid connectivity to on-premises systems without VPN, improving performance and security.
Provides network-layer segmentation complementing identity and data-layer security controls, following defense-in-depth principle.
cons: Private Endpoints add operational complexity: VNet design, routing, DNS configuration, and troubleshooting require network expertise.
Cost increases: Fabric Capacity pricing unchanged but Private Endpoints charge $0.50/hour per endpoint, adding $360/month per capacity.
Hybrid connectivity requires ExpressRoute or Site-to-Site VPN; on-premises connectivity adds complexity and latency.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Create Azure VNet in the same region as Fabric Capacity. 2. In Fabric Workspace Settings, enable Private Endpoints: select Capacity > Network > Enable Private Endpoint. 3. Azure creates Private Link Service and provides Private Endpoint Connection. 4. In your VNet, create Private Endpoint resource: +Create > Private Endpoint, select Fabric service, select subnet, configure DNS integration. 5. Update DNS resolver to map fabric.microsoft.com to private IP (e.g., 10.0.0.5). 6. Test connectivity: connect VM in VNet, query nslookup fabric.microsoft.com (should resolve to private IP). 7. Configure Managed Private Endpoints for Spark: Workspace > Managed Private Endpoints > +New, select target resource (SQL Database, storage), approve in target resource. 8. Configure Network Security Group (NSG): allow only VNet subnets to reach Fabric Private Endpoint, deny internet-routed traffic.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish network security architecture review with IT security and compliance teams. Document VNet design and Private Endpoint locations. Implement bastion hosts for secure VM access instead of public IPs. Enforce NSG rules preventing traffic to public internet. Monitor Private Endpoint connections and deny suspicious outbound attempts. Require change management approval for VNet/NSG changes. Conduct quarterly network segmentation audits.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Financial services firm isolates HR analytics Fabric Capacity on private VNet, restricts outbound traffic to approved SIEM sink via proxy, ensuring no employee data transits internet.
Healthcare provider connects on-premises HRIS system via ExpressRoute to Fabric over private endpoint, enabling analytics without routing PII through public internet or VPN.
Multi-tenant SaaS provider isolates each customer's Fabric Capacity on separate VNet, using managed private endpoints to connect to customer-specific SQL databases on-premises.

referenceLinks.title: Network isolation with managed endpoints - Azure ML
Managed virtual network isolation - Azure ML

patterns.costImplications: Private Endpoint: $0.50/hour ($360/month per endpoint). VNet/NSG: no cost. ExpressRoute (if used): $0.30-0.50/hour depending on bandwidth.

patterns.name: Just-in-Time Privileged Access Management
patterns.summary: Implements Azure Entra ID Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for just-in-time elevation of workspace admin roles, with approval workflows and MFA enforcement.
patterns.description: Privileged Identity Management (PIM) reduces standing permissions, requiring users to request temporary elevation to admin roles. For HR analytics, workspace admins manage data governance policies, sharing, and sensitive configurations. Rather than making users permanent admins (standing privilege), PIM makes them 'Eligible Admins' who must request activation. Requests require approval from compliance officer and MFA authentication. Activation is time-limited (1-8 hours) and audited. Break-glass emergency accounts provide fallback access if primary admins are unavailable. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is required for all privileged actions. Audit logs in Entra ID capture all activation requests, approvals, and actions performed during elevated access. For compliance, PIM demonstrates least-privilege principle and immediate detection of unauthorized privilege escalation attempts.
pros: Reduces standing privilege, dramatically lowering risk of compromise: admin account hack affects only active activation window (1-8 hours) not 365 days.
Requires approval for every elevation, creating human checkpoint preventing unauthorized access.
Complete audit trail of privilege escalation enables detection of unusual elevation patterns and supports compliance audits.
cons: PIM adds friction to emergency access scenarios, requiring approval workflow adds 15-30 minutes to incident response.
Admin users must have Entra ID Premium P2 license, adding cost ~$30-50/user/month.
Misconfigured break-glass accounts (credentials leaked, not rotated) undermine PIM's security benefits.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Ensure Entra ID Premium P2 licensed. 2. In Entra ID > Privileged Identity Management > Fabric Workspace Roles, select 'Workspace Admin' role. 3. Set eligible users: add workspace managers as Eligible (not Permanent) members. 4. Configure activation: require approval, require MFA, set max activation duration to 4 hours. 5. Select approval delegator: compliance officer or security team. 6. Configure notifications: alert on elevations, email approvers. 7. Create break-glass account: dedicate account for emergency access, store credentials in physical safe (not digital). 8. Test activation: Eligible Admin requests activation, approver receives email, activates with MFA. 9. Monitor in Azure Sentinel: create alert for approval denials, unusual activation times, after-hours elevations. 10. Quarterly review: audit PIM logs, revoke unused eligible access.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish PIM governance committee with security, HR, and compliance stakeholders. Document approval process and SLA for activation requests (should be <15 minutes). Implement technical controls enforcing MFA (hardware keys preferred). Rotate break-glass credentials quarterly with multiple stakeholders witnessing. Test break-glass emergency access annually. Review PIM logs monthly for suspicious patterns. Enforce organization-wide policy: no permanent privileged roles, all admins must use PIM.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: HR analytics workspace admin (normally ineligible) requests elevation to investigate unauthorized data export, approver grants 4-hour activation via MFA approval, admin investigates access logs with limited time window.
Data governance officer regularly activates to review sensitivity label assignments and RLS policies, must approve each activation with business justification.
Emergency incident: production Fabric capacity is down, break-glass account holder activates with MFA, gains temporary admin access to restart services, audited and revoked within 1 hour.
patterns.costImplications: Entra ID Premium P2: $30-50/user/month (per admin). PIM: included in Premium P2. Hardware MFA keys: $20-50 per unit.

patterns.name: Dual-Approval Change Management Pipeline
patterns.summary: Enforces dual-approval (business and governance) before production pipeline deployment, implementing four-eyes principle via Azure DevOps gates.
patterns.description: Four-eyes principle requires two independent approvals before changes to production systems. For HR analytics, changes to data pipelines (transformations, data quality rules, refresh schedules) can affect payroll, benefits, or compliance analytics. Requiring dual approval ensures both business correctness (HR manager) and governance compliance (data governance officer) are verified. Azure DevOps Deployment Pipelines implement gates between environments: development -> staging -> production. Staging gate requires business approval from HR analytics owner confirming transformations are correct. Production gate requires data governance approval confirming RLS policies, data lineage, and compliance are maintained. Pull request reviews enforce code quality and documentation before merge to main. Git branching strategy separates features, requiring peer review. Change log automatically documents approvers, timestamp, and change summary. Rejection of changes includes audit trail for compliance.
pros: Enforces dual approval, preventing single-person errors and unauthorized changes to critical analytics.
Creates audit trail proving compliance with change control requirements, supporting SOX, HIPAA audits.
Improves quality by requiring peer review before production deployment.
cons: Adds cycle time: waiting for two approvers can delay urgent fixes (typical cycle time 24-48 hours).
Requires both approvers to be available; absence of approver blocks deployment.
False sense of security if approvers don't actually review changes carefully.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Set up Azure DevOps project with Git repo for Fabric pipeline definitions. 2. Configure branch policy on main: require pull request reviews, minimum 2 approvers (business + governance), status checks passing. 3. Create staging deployment pipeline: trigger on PR approval, deploy to staging environment. 4. Add pre-deployment gate before staging: auto-approve (runs tests, validates syntax). 5. Add pre-deployment gate before production: manual approval, allow only specific users (data governance team). Require justification/description. 6. Create business approval gate: HR manager reviews transformations, confirms correctness. 7. Track approvals: Azure DevOps automatically logs timestamp, approver identity, comments. 8. Reject approvals include mandatory reason. 9. Create dashboard: count deployments, approval time metrics, rejection rates. 10. Quarterly review: analyze approval bottlenecks, optimize process.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Define who can request, approve, and reject changes: business sponsor (business approval), data governance (compliance approval). Document approval criteria: business approval verifies transformations match requirements, governance approval verifies RLS, lineage, data quality, compliance. Implement escalation path for urgent changes (e.g., 24-hour SLA for critical bug fixes). Audit approval logs monthly. Require documented change rationale in pull request. Disallow approval from same person who submitted change (dual-approval enforced technically).
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Data engineer submits PR changing employee salary aggregation logic (e.g., fixing bonus calculation). Business sponsor (payroll manager) approves confirming calculation is correct. Data governance approves confirming aggregation preserves privacy (k>=5). Production deployment occurs only after both approvals.

New HR dataset onboarded to Lakehouse: ingestion pipeline PR submitted. Business sponsor approves confirming data matches HRIS system. Data governance approves confirming sensitivity labels assigned, lineage documented. Both approvals required before prod activation.
Urgent hotfix: salary dashboard showing incorrect totals. Change submitted with 'urgent' flag. Both approvers pinged, typically respond within 2-4 hours. Production deployment after both approvals.

referenceLinks.title: Change approval settings for access packages

patterns.costImplications: Azure DevOps: free for up to 5 users/project. Git: no cost. Slack/Teams notifications: included. Process overhead: ~1-2 hours per change for approvals.

patterns.name: Cross-Border Data Residency Isolation
patterns.summary: Isolates employee data by geography using multi-geo Fabric capacities, ensuring Canadian employee data remains in Canada and US data in US, with aggregate-only cross-border reporting.
patterns.description: Cross-border data residency ensures that employee personal data is stored and processed only within specified geographies per regulatory requirements. For multinational HR analytics, Canadian employees' data (salary, SSN, benefits) must remain in Canada due to PIPEDA. US data must remain in US due to state regulations and employer obligations. Fabric multi-geo capacity configuration assigns workspaces to specific regions; OneLake data is geo-pinned to that region. Cross-border reporting uses aggregate-only views (k-anonymity approach) ensuring individual records cannot be queried across borders. Data sharing uses Shortcuts with filtering, preventing cross-border record-level export. Periodic reviews ensure no data has migrated across borders. Audit logs track cross-border access attempts.
pros: Meets regulatory data residency requirements (PIPEDA, GDPR, local laws) preventing costly compliance violations.
Enables multi-country operations with confidence that data stays in authorized regions.
Provides operational resilience: country-level outage affects only that region's operations, not global.
cons: Multi-region capacities increase costs ~2-3x vs. single-region (separate capacity per region).
Analytics across regions requires federated queries or cross-border aggregates; complex joins are impossible.
Data migration for employee moves (e.g., employee relocates from Canada to US) requires careful handling: old records deletion or transfer.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Create separate Fabric Capacity per geography: Canadian Capacity (Canada Central region), US Capacity (US East 2 region). 2. Create separate workspaces per geography: 'HR-Analytics-CA' in Canadian capacity, 'HR-Analytics-US' in US capacity. 3. Ingest employee data to geo-pinned Lakehouse: Canadian employee table in Canada workspace, US employee table in US workspace. 4. For cross-border reporting, create aggregate-only views: SELECT department, YEAR(dob) as year_of_birth, COUNT(*) as employee_count FROM employees WHERE country='CA' GROUP BY department, YEAR(dob) HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5. 5. Create federated semantic models: Power BI connects to Canadian and US aggregate views, combines aggregates (no record-level data). 6. Prevent cross-border shortcuts: Workspace Sharing > restrict shortcuts to same-region workspaces. 7. Audit cross-border access: Log Analytics tracks queries across regions, alert on suspicious activity. 8. Data migration procedure: terminating employee, update country field, archive to historical table in original region, do not migrate raw records.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Establish data residency governance committee with legal, compliance, and HR stakeholders per country. Document residency requirements by jurisdiction (PIPEDA, GDPR, state laws). Implement technical enforcement: prevent shortcuts crossing regions, audit logs for attempted cross-border access. Quarterly audit: verify no employee data exists in wrong region. Data transfer agreements: document how employee relocations are handled. Secure deletion: ensure migrated data is permanently deleted from source region.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Canadian bank with Canadian HQ + US subsidiary: employees are separated by capacity/workspace. Canadian headquarters views all Canadian employee analytics in Canadian workspace (compliant with PIPEDA). US subsidiary views US employee analytics in US workspace. Joint reporting uses aggregates: total Canadian headcount + total US headcount, no cross-border record-level joins.

Multinational tech company with employees in Canada, US, and EU: separate capacities for each region. Annual global report aggregates at country-group level: 'Canada 5000 employees, US 10000 employees, EU 3000 employees' without exposing individual records.

Employee relocation: Canadian employee transfers to US. Old record in Canadian Lakehouse is soft-deleted (marked inactive), no migration to US workspace. US onboarding creates new record in US workspace for same employee.

referenceLinks.title: What is the EU Data Boundary?

patterns.costImplications: Multi-region Fabric Capacity: 2-3x capacity cost (separate capacity per region). Premium P1 capacity: $16/hour per region. Cross-border OneLake replication: $0.02/GB/month.

patterns.name: Fabric Data Agent for HR Self-Service
patterns.summary: Native Fabric Data Agent converts natural language questions to SQL, DAX, or KQL queries for HR self-service, enabling employees to query compensation, benefits, and organizational data securely.
patterns.description: Fabric Data Agents are native Fabric items that convert natural language questions into structured queries (SQL, DAX, KQL) against connected data sources. For HR self-service, employees can ask questions like 'What is my salary band?' or 'How many direct reports does my manager have?' and the Data Agent retrieves answers respecting row-level and column-level security automatically. Data Agents can connect up to 5 data sources simultaneously (Lakehouse, Warehouse, Power BI Semantic Models, KQL databases, ontologies) and run under the user's Microsoft Entra ID identity, automatically enforcing existing workspace and data permissions. Unlike RAG chatbots that require custom engineering, Data Agents are low-code and leverage Fabric's native query capabilities. Supports custom instructions (up to 15,000 characters) and example queries for few-shot learning. Has a publish/share lifecycle with draft and published versions.
pros: Native Fabric item type reduces custom development overhead vs RAG chatbots.
Runs under user identity so RLS and CLS enforced automatically without retrieval-layer complexity.
Supports 5 concurrent data sources enabling rich queries across org hierarchy, compensation, benefits.
Custom instructions and few-shot learning reduce hallucinations without guardrail overhead.
Low-code interface reduces time-to-value vs custom LLM implementations.
Read-only queries prevent accidental data modification.
Can ground responses in unstructured data (PDFs, docs) via Azure AI Search integration.
cons: Requires F2+ capacity (expensive for large orgs) or P1+ with Fabric enabled.
Cross-geo AI processing/storing must be enabled, raising data residency considerations.
Query complexity limited compared to hand-optimized SQL; may be slow for complex analytical joins.
Limited customization vs building custom LLM agents.
Dependency on Fabric governance; organizational changes require Data Agent updates.
May hallucinate for edge case queries without sufficient examples.
patterns.usageInstructions: 1. Create new Data Agent in workspace (right-click workspace > Create > Data Agent). 2. Connect up to 5 data sources: select Lakehouse for org structure, Warehouse for compensation/benefits, semantic model for aggregates. 3. Verify F2+ capacity is enabled; enable cross-geo AI processing. 4. Add custom instructions (max 15,000 chars): 'Employees see only personal and team compensation data. Managers see direct reports. HR sees all. Always respond in plain English.' 5. Add example queries with expected results: 'What is my salary band?' -> [expected output], 'List my direct reports.' -> [expected output]. 6. Test with draft version: ask questions, validate security enforcement. 7. Publish to workspace for sharing. 8. Grant workspace access to employees; Data Agent inherits permissions. 9. Monitor query logs for slow/failed queries; update examples if needed.
patterns.governanceConsiderations: Data Agent inherits RLS/CLS from semantic models and warehouse views automatically; verify enforcement in dev before production. Restrict Data Agent creation to HR analytics team (workspace contributor role). Custom instructions must reference approved data classification (public, internal, sensitive). Example queries must use anonymized/redacted values, never actual employee data. Audit Data Agent access logs monthly, especially for modified queries. If ontologies are used, ensure ontology governance aligns with HR data governance. Document query volume limits per user to prevent runaway costs. For cross-border orgs, enable cross-geo AI processing only with legal/compliance review.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: HR Self-Service Portal: employees ask 'What are my benefits eligibility dates?' or 'What is my current salary band?' Agent queries benefits and compensation Warehouse views, respecting employee RLS.
Manager Self-Service: managers ask 'What is my team's average tenure?' or 'List headcount by level in my org.' Agent queries org hierarchy and employee Lakehouse, enforcing manager RLS.
Talent Acquisition: recruiters ask 'How many employees have Python skills?' or 'Show me flight engineering leads.' Agent queries skills and title data, respecting recruiter workspace access.
Compensation Planning: HR asks 'What is 2024 salary spend by department?' Agent aggregates payroll Warehouse, enforcing HR-only access.
Learning and Development: ask 'Show training completion by competency last quarter.' Agent queries learning records and aggregates by department.
patterns.costImplications: Requires F2+ capacity ($100-3000/month depending on org size). Cross-geo AI processing may incur additional costs. Usage-based LLM costs depend on query volume.

patterns.name: Workday HRIS Data Ingestion Pipeline
patterns.summary: Ingests HR core data from Workday into OneLake via Fabric Data Factory/Dataflow Gen2 using Report-as-a-Service (RaaS) API and SOAP/REST connectors.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes a robust pipeline for extracting employee records, organizational hierarchy, compensation data, benefits information, and job history from Workday into OneLake. It leverages Workday RaaS APIs and REST/SOAP connectors within Fabric Data Factory or Dataflow Gen2. The pattern supports both full load and incremental load strategies, with data landing in a medallion architecture: Bronze layer receives raw extracts, Silver layer applies data quality rules and standardization, and Gold layer produces business-ready HR master data tables. Change Data Capture techniques enable efficient incremental loading of only changed records.
pros: Direct API integration reduces reliance on manual exports.
Supports both full and incremental loads for efficiency.
Medallion architecture enforces data quality at each stage.
Enables real-time or scheduled refresh based on business needs.
Centralizes all HR master data in OneLake for downstream analytics.
cons: Workday API complexity requires specialized knowledge.
Rate limiting on Workday APIs may impact full-load performance.
Ongoing maintenance needed as Workday updates schemas.
PII volume requires robust governance and masking strategies.
Initial mapping and testing can be time-consuming.
usageInstructions: 1. Set up Workday service account with Report-as-a-Service permissions.
2. Create Data Factory linked service for Workday RaaS API.
3. Define Bronze Lakehouse tables for raw employee, compensation, org hierarchy, benefits, and job history data.
4. Build Dataflow Gen2 or pipeline activities to extract full initial load.
5. Implement incremental load logic using Workday change indicators or watermark columns.
6. Create Silver layer transformations to standardize employee IDs, dates, and categorical values.
7. Build Gold layer views merging employee, compensation, and org hierarchy.
8. Apply sensitivity labels to PII columns (SSN, salary, email).
9. Schedule refresh cadence (daily, weekly, or event-driven).
10. Monitor pipeline SLAs and Workday API quota usage.
governanceConsiderations: Apply dynamic data masking or PII tokenization to salary and benefits data in lower environments.
Enforce RLS at Gold layer to restrict compensation visibility to authorized HR/Finance users.
Maintain audit logs of all extractions from Workday for compliance.
Use sensitivity labels (Highly Confidential) on employee personal data.
Implement data retention policies aligning with HR data archival requirements.
Validate data freshness and completeness daily; alert on extraction failures.
Document business rules for employee status categorization and job classification.
Segregate active vs. historical employee data to enable time-travel analytics.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Employee master analytics: headcount trends, department distribution, tenure analysis.
Compensation equity analysis: salary benchmarking, pay gap identification, job-level costing.
Organizational network analysis: reporting lines, span of control, cross-functional connections.
Benefits utilization tracking: enrollment trends, plan costs, coverage gaps.
Job history and career progression analysis: promotion rates, tenure in role, succession pipeline.
patterns.costImplications: Compute costs vary with extraction frequency; incremental loads reduce ingestion time. Workday API licensing may apply. Monitor OneLake storage for growth.
patterns.connectorInfo: No native Fabric connector for Workday. Use Data Factory with REST API connector to call Workday RaaS/SOAP endpoints, or ingest via EIB flat-file exports to OneLake. Third-party options include CData or Workato connectors.

patterns.name: Qualtrics Survey & Experience Data Ingestion
patterns.summary: Pulls employee engagement surveys, pulse surveys, and 360 feedback from Qualtrics API into OneLake with PII handling for open-text responses.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes a pipeline to extract employee survey data from Qualtrics, including engagement surveys, pulse surveys, 360-degree feedback, and eNPS assessments. The pipeline retrieves response-level data, survey metadata, question hierarchies, and scoring logic via Qualtrics XM API. It handles sensitive data in open-text responses through PII detection and masking. Data flows through medallion architecture with Bronze landing raw responses, Silver standardizing response formats and redacting sensitive feedback, and Gold creating aggregated survey scores and sentiment analysis.
pros: Rich API provides structured response data and survey metadata.
Enables measurement of employee engagement and pulse sentiment over time.
Open-text response data enables sentiment and text analytics.
Qualtrics integrations with other HR platforms simplify cross-dataset correlation.
Response-level granularity allows for demographic cohort analysis.
cons: Open-text responses contain significant PII requiring careful handling.
Qualtrics API rate limits may slow extraction of large response volumes.
Survey design changes in Qualtrics can break extraction logic.
Cross-survey comparison requires careful question mapping.
Sentiment analysis on open-text responses requires NLP models with HR-domain tuning.
usageInstructions: 1. Create Qualtrics API token with read permissions for surveys and responses.
2. Set up Data Factory linked service for Qualtrics XM API.
3. List all active surveys and capture survey IDs, question IDs, and response scales.
4. Create Bronze Lakehouse tables for raw responses, survey definitions, and response metadata.
5. Build pipeline to extract responses incrementally (using response creation date filter).
6. Implement PII detection on open-text fields (regex patterns for names, emails, phone numbers).
7. Create Silver layer to redact or tokenize detected PII in open-text columns.
8. Calculate survey response rates and time-to-respond metrics in Silver.
9. Build Gold layer views with aggregated survey scores by department, tenure band, and role.
10. Optional: Apply sentiment analysis or topic modeling to open-text feedback in Gold.
11. Configure alerts for low response rates or survey completion failures.
governanceConsiderations: Classify survey responses as Confidential or Highly Confidential due to PII and honest feedback.
Implement RLS to restrict individual response visibility to HR and authorized managers.
Apply PII tokenization to open-text fields before storing in lower environments.
Maintain anonymity of survey respondents; never expose individual responses in dashboards.
Document PII detection patterns and regularly audit for missed personally identifiable content.
Enforce data retention aligned with employee tenure (e.g., survey data deleted after employee exit).
Log all access to survey data for compliance audits.
Implement consent tracking if surveys are optional or require explicit participation consent.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Employee engagement measurement: quarterly engagement scores, trend analysis by department.
Pulse survey analytics: rapid feedback on organizational changes, leadership transitions, office reopening.
360-degree feedback aggregation: peer and manager feedback trends, leadership development insights.
eNPS (Employee Net Promoter Score) tracking: likelihood to recommend company to others.
Sentiment analysis: identifying themes in open-text feedback (e.g., 'manager support', 'work-life balance').
Engagement drivers analysis: correlation between engagement scores and retention/attrition.
prerequisites: API access with survey administration permissions
patterns.costImplications: Qualtrics API calls scale with response volume. OneLake storage scales with response history. PII detection/masking may incur additional Fabric compute costs.

patterns.name: Percipio Learning Platform Data Ingestion
patterns.summary: Ingests xAPI/SCORM learning records, course completions, skill assessments, and learning paths from Percipio (Skillsoft) into OneLake.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes a pipeline to extract learning and development data from Percipio by Skillsoft, including xAPI (Experience API) statement data, SCORM completion records, course and assignment progress, skill assessment scores, and learning path enrollments. The pipeline leverages Percipio REST APIs and SFTP feeds for bulk exports. Data flows through medallion architecture: Bronze stores raw xAPI statements and enrollment records, Silver normalizes xAPI JSON payloads and joins with learner demographics, Gold creates learner journey and skills proficiency views for analytics.
pros: xAPI standard enables detailed learning event tracking (viewed, started, completed, scored).
Skill assessment data enables workforce skills mapping and gap analysis.
Learning path tracking enables measurement of learning program effectiveness.
Percipio integrates with major HRIS platforms for learner master data enrichment.
Bulk SFTP export option provides cost-effective data movement.
cons: xAPI JSON payloads are complex and require deep parsing logic.
SCORM completion records may not capture all learning events (e.g., paused, resumed).
xAPI data volumes can be very large for organizations with thousands of learners.
Skill assessment scoring differs by course; requires domain-specific interpretation.
Limited standardization across learning content providers makes cross-platform correlation difficult.
usageInstructions: 1. Set up Percipio API credentials (OAuth 2.0) with learning data export permissions.
2. Create Data Factory linked service for Percipio REST API.
3. Define Bronze Lakehouse tables for xAPI statements (raw JSON), course enrollments, and skill assessments.
4. Build pipeline to extract xAPI statements incrementally (querying by timestamp filters).
5. Use Spark Notebooks to parse xAPI JSON payloads and flatten nested structures.
6. Join learner IDs from Percipio with employee master data (from Workday).
7. Create Silver layer views normalizing xAPI verbs (completed, passed, scored) and object types (course, assessment).
8. Build Gold layer learning journey tables with learner enrollment, start/completion dates, and assessment scores.
9. Create skills proficiency views aggregating assessment scores by skill and learner.
10. Optional: Build learning compliance dashboard tracking mandatory course completion rates.
11. Schedule daily or weekly incremental loads based on learning activity volume.
governanceConsiderations: Classify learning data as Confidential (assessment scores and learning preferences are sensitive).
Implement RLS to restrict individual learner progress visibility to their managers and learning administrators.
Document xAPI parsing logic and maintain version history as Percipio updates content structure.
Align data retention with training program lifecycle (e.g., retain assessment data for 3 years post-completion).
Validate xAPI statement completeness; monitor for missing or malformed records.
Correlate learning engagement with performance and retention to measure L&D program effectiveness.
Apply sensitivity labels to assessment scores and learning gaps data.
Ensure compliance with training certification requirements (e.g., document completion for regulated training).
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Learning engagement tracking: course completion rates, time-to-completion, learner activity trends.
Skills proficiency measurement: workforce skills by department, skills gaps for talent planning.
Training program ROI: correlation between course completion and promotion, retention, and performance.
Compliance and certification tracking: mandatory training completion rates, certification renewals.
Personalized learning recommendations: suggest courses based on current skills and career path.
Succession planning: identify high-engagement learners as succession candidates for leadership roles.
prerequisites: API access and xAPI statement export permissions
patterns.costImplications: xAPI data volume drives OneLake storage costs. Spark Notebook compute for JSON parsing scales with learner count. Monitor SFTP bandwidth if using bulk exports.

patterns.name: Pluralsight Skills & Learning Data Ingestion
patterns.summary: Ingests Skill IQ assessments, course progress, channel analytics, and role IQ data from Pluralsight API or SFTP exports into OneLake.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes a pipeline to extract learning and skills data from Pluralsight, including Skill IQ assessment results and skill levels, course start/completion progress, channel and library analytics, role IQ readiness assessments, and learning path enrollments. The pipeline leverages Pluralsight REST APIs or scheduled SFTP file exports. Data flows through medallion architecture: Bronze stores raw assessment results and enrollment records, Silver normalizes skill levels and maps them to standard competency frameworks, Gold creates skills inventories and capability readiness views for workforce planning.
pros: Skill IQ assessments provide validated skills measurements and certification readiness.
Role IQ benchmarks learner against industry role standards for skills gap identification.
Course-level learning progress enables measurement of learning pace and engagement.
Pluralsight channels enable department or team-level learning analytics.
API and SFTP options provide flexibility in data movement approach.
cons: Skill IQ results are point-in-time snapshots; repeated assessments required for trend analysis.
Pluralsight role taxonomy may not align with internal job classification systems.
Assessment fatigue if learners are over-assessed; requires careful cadence planning.
Course difficulty and duration vary widely; simple completion counts are insufficient for impact.
Pluralsight data alone cannot measure on-the-job skill application.
usageInstructions: 1. Set up Pluralsight API credentials or SFTP account for data exports.
2. Create Data Factory linked service for Pluralsight API or SFTP endpoint.
3. Define Bronze Lakehouse tables for raw skill assessments, role IQ results, enrollments, and course progress.
4. Build pipeline to extract assessments incrementally (weekly or monthly snapshots).
5. Create lookup table mapping Pluralsight skills to internal competency framework.
6. In Silver layer, normalize skill levels (beginner to expert) and add internal job family mappings.
7. Build role readiness metrics (e.g., percentage of required skills at target level).
8. Create Gold layer views with learner skills inventory, skill trends over time, and role readiness.
9. Join with Workday employee master for department and job level context.
10. Build skills heatmap showing department capability by role and critical skill.
11. Schedule weekly or monthly incremental loads to track skill progression.
governanceConsiderations: Classify skills and assessment data as Confidential (skills gaps may affect career decisions).
Implement RLS restricting individual skill results to the learner, their manager, and talent development teams.
Document skill level definitions and Pluralsight assessment methodologies for transparency.
Validate skill assessment frequency; balance insight gathering with assessment fatigue risk.
Maintain historical skill assessments for trend analysis and career progression tracking.
Align skills taxonomy with role architecture; update mappings when roles change.
Apply sensitivity labels to role readiness results (low readiness may be sensitive).
Monitor for bias in skills assessment results across demographic groups.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Skills inventory: workforce capability by department, role, and skill.
Skills gap analysis: critical skills shortages, upskilling priorities for strategic initiatives.
Role readiness assessment: percentage of team ready for new technologies or business initiatives.
Learning effectiveness: correlation between Skill IQ improvement and performance/promotion.
Retention and engagement: skilled learners may show higher engagement and lower attrition.
Succession planning: identify learners with high skills growth trajectory as future leaders.
prerequisites: Pluralsight Skills (formerly A Cloud Guru) subscription
API access or SFTP export permissions
patterns.costImplications: Skill assessment volume drives API calls and potential quota overages. OneLake storage for long-term skill history. Compute for skills taxonomy mapping.

patterns.name: FileX Managed File Transfer Integration
patterns.summary: Uses FileX or similar managed file transfer (MFT) tools for secure file movement into OneLake SFTP landing zones with automated validation and virus scanning.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes a secure file transfer mechanism for vendors and third-party systems that only support flat file exports (CSV, Excel, JSON, XML). It leverages FileX, Tresorit Secure File Transfer, or similar managed file transfer platforms to route files into OneLake SFTP landing zones. The pattern includes automated file arrival detection, schema validation, virus scanning, and orchestration into Lakehouse Bronze tables via Data Factory pipelines. It is ideal for legacy HR systems, payroll providers, and benefit administrators that cannot support modern APIs.
pros: Enables data ingestion from vendors with limited API capabilities.
MFT platforms provide encryption, audit trails, and compliance certifications.
SFTP landing zones are cost-effective and widely supported by enterprise file systems.
Automated file validation catches schema mismatches before processing.
Virus scanning protects against malware in vendor-supplied files.
cons: Flat file formats lack schema rigor; require careful mapping and type inference.
No change data capture; full files are re-ingested, requiring duplicate detection.
File arrival timing is unpredictable; monitoring and retry logic are essential.
Manual file format changes by vendors can break pipelines.
File-based transfer introduces latency compared to APIs.
usageInstructions: 1. Set up FileX or similar MFT platform account.
2. Create SFTP landing zone in OneLake (or cloud storage referenced from OneLake shortcuts).
3. Configure FileX to route vendor files to the SFTP landing zone.
4. Document expected file naming convention (e.g., VENDOR_YYYYMMDD_FILETYPE.csv).
5. Define schema for each vendor file type (column names, data types, required fields).
6. Create Data Factory pipeline with triggers on SFTP folder arrival events.
7. Implement file validation: check file naming, detect schema changes, validate row counts.
8. Add virus scanning step (if supported by MFT platform) before processing.
9. Build Dataflow Gen2 or Spark activity to parse file and load into Lakehouse Bronze table.
10. Implement duplicate detection and row-level change tracking (use file checksum + row hash).
11. Create audit log table recording file name, arrival time, row count, and processing status.
12. Set up alerts for file arrival failures, schema mismatches, or duplicate thresholds.
13. Archive processed files to cold storage after configurable retention period.
governanceConsiderations: Maintain comprehensive file transfer audit trail (arrival, receipt, processing, archival dates).
Encrypt files in transit (MFT platform requirement) and at rest in OneLake.
Restrict SFTP access to authorized Data Factory service principal only.
Document data lineage: which vendor file maps to which Lakehouse table.
Implement PII handling: if files contain sensitive data (SSN, salary), apply masking in Bronze.
Archive files according to data retention policies (e.g., 7 years for payroll files).
Monitor file volume and quality; alert on unexpected schema or completeness changes.
Require vendor sign-off on file format changes before pipeline updates.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Payroll file ingestion: monthly payroll exports from ADP, Ceridian, or local payroll systems.
Benefits data integration: enrollment, cost, and claims files from benefits administrators.
Attendance/timekeeping: timesheet exports from legacy time-tracking systems.
Recruitment data: offer letter and background check results from recruiting platforms.
Survey responses: exported survey files from survey tools with limited API support.
prerequisites: FileX or alternative MFT platform license
SFTP capability in OneLake or connected cloud storage
patterns.costImplications: FileX or MFT platform licensing. OneLake storage for file archives. Data Factory activity costs for validation and parsing.

patterns.name: Git-Based Version Control for Fabric Artifacts
patterns.summary: Implements Git integration in Fabric (Azure DevOps or GitHub) for versioning notebooks, pipelines, and semantic models with branch strategies and CI/CD deployment.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes source code version control and continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) for Fabric artifacts. It leverages Azure DevOps or GitHub as the source of truth for Spark notebooks, Data Factory pipelines, Power BI semantic models, and Lakehouse table definitions. The pattern implements branch strategies (development, testing, production), automated testing on pull requests, code reviews, and automated deployments to workspace environments. Git integration enables reproducibility, auditability, and safe rollback of analytics changes.
pros: Git history provides full traceability of all analytics and data changes.
Branch strategies enable safe testing before production deployment.
Code review (pull request) gates improve quality and knowledge sharing.
Automated CI/CD reduces manual deployment errors.
Easy rollback to previous versions if production issues occur.
Enables multiple data scientists and engineers to collaborate on shared notebooks and pipelines.
cons: Git history can become large for binary Power BI semantic models (.pbix files).
Merge conflicts in notebooks require careful resolution to avoid code corruption.
Fabric Git integration has limitations (e.g., partial workspace sync support).
Requires discipline to commit regularly and write meaningful commit messages.
Testing frameworks for notebooks and pipelines are less mature than software engineering.
usageInstructions: 1. Create Azure DevOps repository or GitHub repository for Fabric workspace.
2. Configure Fabric workspace Git integration (Settings > Git > Connect).
3. Choose remote branch strategy: feature branches for development, main/master for production.
4. Create branch protection rules on main: require PR review and passing CI checks.
5. Document notebook and pipeline naming conventions for discoverability.
6. Implement testing strategy: unit tests for Spark transformations, pipeline validation tests.
7. Configure Azure Pipelines or GitHub Actions for CI/CD: run tests on PR, deploy to dev/test on merge.
8. Create deployment pipeline stages: dev (auto), test (auto), prod (manual approval).
9. Document commit message conventions (e.g., 'feat: add employee retention scoring', 'fix: correct salary calculation').
10. Establish code review guidelines and assign code owners for critical transformations.
11. Schedule regular backups of workspace to secondary Git branch for disaster recovery.
12. Monitor Git sync errors and audit logs for unauthorized changes.
governanceConsiderations: Enforce branch protection and require peer review (minimum 1-2 approvers) for production changes.
Use Git commit signing (GPG keys) to verify authorship of critical changes.
Audit all Git commits and deployments; maintain immutable audit log.
Restrict push permissions to main branch; allow only CI/CD service principals.
Version-pin dependencies in notebooks (library versions, Spark versions) to ensure reproducibility.
Implement automated testing to validate data transformations before production deployment.
Document data lineage in Git commits: which notebooks/pipelines modify which tables.
Maintain Git history retention aligned with data governance policies (e.g., 2+ year retention for audit).
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: HR analytics development: track changes to attrition, compensation, and engagement models.
Workforce planning models: version iterations of ML models for headcount forecasting.
Data quality monitoring: maintain transformation and validation logic changes over time.
Compliance reporting automation: version and audit changes to regulatory reporting queries.
Ad-hoc analysis notebooks: maintain shared analysis templates versioned for reproducibility.
prerequisites: Fabric workspace with Git integration capability
Azure Pipelines or GitHub Actions for CI/CD
patterns.costImplications: No additional cost beyond Fabric licenses. Azure Pipelines/GitHub Actions compute for CI/CD may incur minimal costs depending on build frequency.

patterns.name: Alation Data Catalog Integration
patterns.summary: Integrates Alation as enterprise data catalog alongside Fabric/Purview for data discovery, business glossary, stewardship workflows, and lineage visualization.
patterns.description: This pattern establishes Alation as a complementary enterprise data catalog working alongside Fabric's native Purview integration. Alation provides rich data discovery experience, collaborative business glossary (domains, terms, stewardship), trust flags and endorsements, and query log analysis for usage patterns. Metadata flows bidirectionally: Fabric Purview feeds technical lineage and asset information to Alation; Alation feeds business context, data stewardship assignments, and certification status back to Fabric. This hybrid approach combines Fabric's native data governance with Alation's discovery and curation capabilities.
pros: Alation provides superior data discovery UX and collaborative glossary compared to Purview alone.
Bidirectional metadata sync reduces manual data entry and maintains consistency.
Trust flags and endorsements highlight high-quality datasets for self-service analytics.
Query log analysis reveals data asset usage patterns and hidden dependencies.
Business glossary serves as source of truth for KPI definitions and metric calculations.
Data stewardship workflow assignment distributes ownership accountability.
cons: Alation + Purview + Fabric creates three governance interfaces; requires clear role separation.
Metadata sync must be carefully orchestrated to avoid conflicts.
Alation licensing adds cost on top of Fabric and Purview.
Data stewards require training on multiple interfaces (Alation + Purview + Power BI).
Query log analysis can identify sensitive queries revealing business strategy.
usageInstructions: 1. Deploy Alation instance (cloud or on-premises) with Fabric/OneLake connector.
2. Configure Purview metadata export to Alation via API or connector.
3. In Alation, set up data domains (HR Analytics, Talent Management, Payroll) as organizational structure.
4. Create business glossary: define HR metrics (attrition, engagement, pay ratio) with domain owners.
5. Assign data stewards to critical Lakehouse tables and Warehouse schemas.
6. Enable query log analysis: connect to Azure SQL Server query logs for usage insights.
7. Create data quality scorecards: freshness, completeness, accuracy metrics per dataset.
8. Build trust badges: endorse high-quality, well-documented datasets.
9. Configure lineage visualization: show data flow from Workday → Bronze → Silver → Gold → Power BI.
10. Create certification workflow: data stewards approve datasets before release to self-service.
11. Integrate Alation with Slack/Teams: publish weekly data quality and governance updates.
12. Train analysts to search Alation first before requesting data access from HR.
governanceConsiderations: Define clear boundaries: Alation handles business glossary and discovery; Purview handles technical lineage and compliance.
Maintain stewardship assignments in Alation; sync to Purview for certification status.
Monitor query logs for sensitive queries (e.g., executive compensation data) and implement additional access controls.
Establish business glossary governance: domain-owner-led review and approval for new glossary terms.
Classify trust levels (endorsed, certified, draft) and make visible in Power BI for self-service consumers.
Implement audit trail: log glossary changes, stewardship assignments, and certification decisions.
Regular reconciliation between Alation and Purview metadata to catch drift.
Use Alation certification as prerequisite for dataset use in regulated reports.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Employee dataset discovery: analysts search for headcount, compensation, performance data with business context.
Attrition metric definition: business glossary defines attrition (12-month voluntary terminations) and calculation method.
Compensation equity audit: data steward certifies salary dataset completeness and confidentiality controls.
Org health scorecard: lineage shows data flow from HRIS → Bronze → Silver → KPI → executive dashboard.
Data quality monitoring: query log shows heavy access to specific departments, triggering investigation of data freshness.
prerequisites: Fabric Purview configured and metadata flowing
patterns.costImplications: Alation licensing (per-user or organization-wide). Integration/connector setup and support. Governance team effort for glossary management.

patterns.name: Informatica IDMC Data Governance Integration
patterns.summary: Uses Informatica Intelligent Data Management Cloud (IDMC) for data quality, master data management (MDM), and data integration with Fabric via connectors/APIs.
patterns.description: This pattern integrates Informatica Intelligent Data Management Cloud (IDMC) as an enterprise data governance platform alongside Fabric. Informatica provides AI-powered data quality rules (CLAIRE), data profiling, data cleansing, and master data management (MDM) for employee golden records. IDMC connectors enable bidirectional data movement between Workday, SAP, Fabric Lakehouse, and other HR systems. CLAIRE AI learns data patterns and automatically detects quality anomalies, missing values, and outliers. MDM capabilities consolidate employee records across multiple HRIS systems (Workday + legacy systems) into a single source of truth.
pros: CLAIRE AI-powered data quality rules adapt to data changes without manual rule updates.
MDM consolidates employee data across multiple HRIS systems into golden records.
Informatica connectors enable tight integration with Fabric data factory pipelines.
Data profiling identifies data quality issues before they impact analytics.
Cleansing capabilities enable consistent employee ID, location, and job title formatting.
Governance metadata flows into Purview for integrated compliance reporting.
cons: Informatica licensing cost is significant, especially for enterprise deployments.
CLAIRE AI requires historical data and time to learn patterns; initial false positives common.
MDM implementation requires careful master record definitions and matching rules.
Informatica and Fabric have overlapping capabilities; choosing which tool owns each function requires planning.
Data stewards require training on Informatica interfaces; knowledge transfer overhead.
usageInstructions: 1. Deploy Informatica IDMC cloud instance with data quality and MDM modules.
2. Configure IDMC connectors for Workday, Fabric Lakehouse, and legacy HRIS systems.
3. Define data profiling rules for employee tables: check completeness, uniqueness, format.
4. Create data quality metrics: percentage of records with valid email, phone, employment status.
5. Set up CLAIRE AI profiling: run auto-profile on employee master to learn value distributions.
6. Define data cleansing rules: standardize names (capitalization), locations (state abbreviations), job titles.
7. Create MDM hub for employee golden records: define matching rules (SSN + name + DOB).
8. Build data factory pipeline: extract from Workday → IDMC for profiling/cleansing → Fabric Lakehouse Bronze.
9. Configure exception handling: flag records failing quality thresholds (e.g., >5% null in required fields).
10. Set up data quality dashboards: daily quality metrics by data source and quality rule.
11. Integrate Informatica metadata with Purview: quality scores and lineage visible in Fabric UI.
12. Schedule regular MDM reconciliation: identify duplicate employee records and merge recommendations.
governanceConsiderations: Define data quality SLAs: e.g., '98% of employee records pass validation before load to Gold layer'.
Maintain audit trail of cleansing transformations: document why fields were changed.
Implement MDM governance: assign stewards to resolve conflicting golden record attributes.
Monitor CLAIRE AI anomaly detection for false positives; refine model as needed.
Classify data quality issues by severity: critical (blocks load), warning (may impact analysis), informational.
Integrate data quality rules with PII masking: cleansing must preserve sensitivity labels.
Document master record definitions: which source system is authoritative for each employee attribute.
Validate cleansing rules do not inadvertently mask real data variance (e.g., two valid address formats).
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Employee master data quality: identify incomplete or inconsistent employee records before analytics.
Compensation analysis: ensure salary and job level data are cleansed and consistent across HR systems.
Organizational network analysis: cleansed manager-employee relationships enable accurate org structure analysis.
Employee lifecycle analytics: cleansed start/end dates and employment status enable accurate tenure calculations.
Cross-system employee matching: MDM resolves duplicate employees across Workday and legacy system.
prerequisites: Data Quality and MDM module licenses
Connectors for Workday and Fabric configured
patterns.costImplications: Informatica IDMC licensing (data quality + MDM modules). Implementation services and data steward training. Ongoing support and rule maintenance.

patterns.name: Self-Service Sandbox Upload Workspace
patterns.summary: Provides a governed but flexible workspace where analysts can upload files (CSV, Excel, JSON) for ad-hoc analysis with auto-schema detection and shortcuts to production data.
patterns.description: This pattern creates a dedicated sandbox workspace where business analysts and data scientists can upload files for exploratory analysis without requiring IT or data engineering support. Files land in a dedicated Lakehouse with automatic schema detection and type inference. The workspace is integrated with main OneLake data via shortcuts, enabling analysts to join sandbox data with production employee, compensation, and survey datasets. Governance includes time-limited retention, PII approval workflows, and audit logging of all uploads and queries.
pros: Enables self-service analytics without IT bottlenecks.
Automatic schema detection reduces data preparation time.
Shortcuts to production data enable cross-joining with HR systems data.
Audit logging maintains governance over exploratory analysis.
Time-limited retention prevents unintended data hoarding.
Isolates experimental work from production systems.
cons: Analysts may upload low-quality or sensitive data without understanding governance requirements.
Auto-schema detection can misclassify columns (e.g., phone numbers as numeric).
Data duplication between sandbox and production increases storage costs.
Requires ongoing monitoring and cleanup of abandoned uploads.
PII approval workflows can slow exploratory analysis cycles.
usageInstructions: 1. Create dedicated Workspace with Sandbox naming (e.g., 'HR Analytics Sandbox').
2. Create Lakehouse with folders: uploads, staging, shared_outputs.
3. Enable Lakehouse auto-schema detection for uploads folder.
4. Create Power Query template for file upload: CSV/Excel → infer schema → Lakehouse table.
5. In Spark Notebooks, build auto-schema detection: inspect first 1000 rows, infer types, alert on anomalies.
6. Create shortcuts to production Gold tables (employee, org, compensation) in sandbox Lakehouse.
7. Build pre-approval data sensitivity questionnaire: 'Does your upload contain PII? Salary? Email addresses?'
8. Route high-sensitivity uploads to HR data steward for approval before processing.
9. Configure 90-day retention: auto-delete old uploads with notifications 30 days before deletion.
10. Enable query audit logging: log all sandbox queries for governance audit trail.
11. Create shared_outputs folder: analysts publish finalized datasets with documentation.
12. Build simple Power BI dashboard showing active uploads, storage usage, and upload trends.
governanceConsiderations: Classify sandbox Lakehouse as Non-Production; apply more relaxed governance than Gold layer.
Implement RLS restricting each analyst's uploads to themselves until explicitly shared.
Require data sensitivity self-assessment before upload; route sensitive data to approval workflow.
Apply PII masking to uploads containing employee personal data unless approved by HR.
Maintain immutable audit log: record who uploaded what, when, and what they queried.
Archive shared_outputs for 1 year after deletion; enable recovery for audit purposes.
Monitor upload patterns: alert if single analyst uploads >1GB in a week.
Require documentation (metadata) for shared outputs: data source, refresh cadence, interpretation guidance.
peopleAnalyticsUseCases: Ad-hoc survey analysis: upload raw survey file, join with employee master, analyze by demographics.
Compensation analysis: upload external benchmark data, compare to internal salary bands.
Org design experimentation: upload proposed org chart changes, analyze span of control impact.
Attrition modeling: upload external labor market data, build correlation models with internal turnover.
Diversity analysis: upload external EEOC benchmark data, compare internal representation.
prerequisites: Governance approval process for PII handling
patterns.costImplications: Storage for temporary uploads. Power BI Pro licenses for participating analysts. Minimal compute if using notebooks for transformation.

patterns.name: Ad-Hoc Insight Creation Engine
patterns.summary: Notebook-based workflow for statistical testing, analysis, and formatted output generation (PDF reports, Excel summaries, PowerPoint slides) with parameterized recurring runs.
patterns.description: This pattern creates a reusable framework for generating ad-hoc HR analytics insights using parameterized Spark Notebooks. Analysts define analysis parameters (e.g., department filter, date range, statistical test type), and notebooks execute statistical tests (t-tests, chi-square, ANOVA, regression) generating formatted output files (PDF reports, Excel dashboards, PowerPoint briefings). Templates exist for common HR analyses: attrition drivers, compensation equity, engagement correlates, org structure optimization. Notebooks are production-quality with error handling, logging, and output validation. They can be scheduled for recurring runs (e.g., monthly compensation equity audit) or triggered on-demand.
pros: Parameterized notebooks enable one-time setup followed by repeated analysis without code changes.
Statistical tests provide scientific rigor to HR insights (vs. dashboards alone).
Formatted output (PDF, Excel, PowerPoint) suitable for executive briefings.
Templates reduce time-to-insight for common analyses.
Scheduled runs enable regular monitoring (e.g., monthly equity audit).
Notebook-based approach enables version control and peer review.
cons: Spark/Python skills required; not accessible to non-technical analysts.
Output file generation (PDF, PowerPoint) requires additional libraries (reportlab, python-pptx).
Statistical test interpretation requires domain expertise; risk of misuse.
Large result datasets (e.g., 100k employee records × 50 statistical tests) can exceed output size limits.
PDF/PowerPoint generation adds processing time and cost.
usageInstructions: 1. Create Spark Notebook template for statistical analysis: template includes imports, logging, error handling.
2. Build parameterized inputs: department_filter, date_range, statistical_test_type, output_format.
3. Implement data loading: query medallion Gold tables (employee, compensation, engagement).
4. Build statistical test functions: t-test (salary by gender), chi-square (attrition by tenure), ANOVA (engagement by dept).
5. Create output formatting: matplotlib plots for distributions, seaborn for correlations.
6. Implement PDF generation: use reportlab to create branded HR Analytics report.
7. Implement Excel generation: create multi-sheet workbook with results, summary statistics, and interpretation.
8. Implement PowerPoint generation: create slide deck with key findings, statistical results, and recommendations.
9. Add validation: check output file sizes, verify all sheets/sections populated, log errors.
10. Create notebook parameters UI: analysts input department, date range, test type without editing code.
11. Schedule recurring runs: e.g., monthly compensation equity analysis, quarterly engagement trends.
12. Output files to shared Lakehouse folder or email distribution list.
